questions.question: Select all that are true about when you mark a node visited in breadth-first search.
choices: You mark a node the instant you enqueue it, so each node enters the frontier exactly once.
Marking on dequeue can let two neighbors enqueue the same node, inflating the queue and miscounting distance.
You should mark a node only after all of its neighbors have been processed.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Mark-on-enqueue keeps each node in the frontier exactly once. Waiting until dequeue lets several frontier neighbors re-enqueue the same node, which inflates the queue and corrupts the layer count.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the minute a cell rots in multi-source rotting-oranges BFS.
choices: It equals the cell's distance to the NEAREST source, because all sources start at minute zero.
It equals the cell's distance to one fixed chosen source.
It equals the total number of rotten cells so far.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Seeding every rotten cell at minute zero makes the pass on which a cell rots its distance to the closest source, not to any single fixed source and not a running count.

questions.question: Select all that are true about what rotting-oranges BFS returns when a fresh orange is walled off by empty cells.
choices: It returns 0, because every reachable orange still rots.
It returns the number of layers that spread.
It returns the count of fresh cells still remaining.
None of the above
questions.explanation: When any cell is still fresh after the queue drains it can never rot, so the answer is -1. None of the three listed outputs is what the algorithm returns.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the cost of the rotting-oranges BFS.
choices: Marking on enqueue makes every node enter the frontier exactly once.
It runs in O(V + E) time, linear in the size of the graph.
On an R by C grid it is O(R times C), because each cell has at most four edges.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Each node and edge is handled a constant number of times because mark-on-enqueue admits each node once, so the traversal is O(V + E), which on a grid is O(R times C).

questions.question: Select all that are true about when Union Find decrements the component count.
choices: It decrements only when a union merges two DIFFERENT roots.
A same-root edge is redundant and must not change the count, because it is a cycle.
It decrements on every edge that is processed.
It decrements each time find is called.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Only a real union of two different roots reduces the count. A same-root edge joins nothing new, it is a cycle, so decrementing on every edge or on every find is wrong.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about Union Find.
choices: It returns the actual shortest path between two nodes.
A bare parent array with no optimizations can degrade find to O(n) in the worst case.
You should decrement the component count on every edge you process.
Path compression makes each successive find slower over time.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Only the second statement is true. Union Find answers connectivity not paths, decrements only on a real union, and path compression makes future finds faster, so the other three are the NOT-true selections.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the graph reactivation container model taught in this lesson.
choices: Swapping the frontier container changes the traversal, and a queue gives BFS for unweighted shortest paths.
A priority queue keyed by distance gives Dijkstra for weighted shortest paths.
A stack gives Union Find.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The frontier container sets the traversal. A queue gives BFS and a priority queue gives Dijkstra, but a stack gives DFS, not Union Find.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the graph problems worked in this lesson.
choices: Dijkstra finalizes a node on its first pop, which is valid only when every edge weight is non-negative.
Kahn's topological sort detects a cycle when the produced order has fewer nodes than the graph.
Two-sum on an array is solved as a graph traversal.
None of the above
questions.explanation: First-pop finalization needs non-negative weights, and Kahn's leaves cycle nodes blocked so the order comes up short. Two-sum is a hashing job, not a graph problem.
